_citation.full_reference: Yajnadevam (Bharath Rao). 2024. An Indus-Sarasvati Cryptanalytic Decipherment. Self-published preprint. Online: yajnadevam.github.io and various forums.
additional_sources: Rao, S. R. 1982. The Decipherment of the Indus Script. New Delhi: Asia Publishing House. ISBN 0-7069-1791-1. (S.R. Rao's competing Sanskrit-logographic decipherment.)
For the Sanskrit lexical anchors used here: Monier-Williams Sanskrit Dictionary (1899); Apte Sanskrit-English Dictionary (1957).
_citation.license: This is a SYNTHETIC reconstruction of a Sanskrit-logographic competing hypothesis built for Phase-30b T3 falsification testing. It does NOT claim to reproduce Yajnadevam's exact 76-allograph alphabetic decipherment, which is alphabetic, not logographic. Instead, it represents the BEST-CASE Sanskrit hypothesis: each anchored sign is mapped to its most plausible Sanskrit lexical equivalent given the iconographic context. This gives the Sanskrit hypothesis maximum benefit of the doubt for the iconographic-anchor test. Reference use only.
_citation.compiled_by: Glossa-Lab Phase-30b T3 (synthetic Sanskrit-logographic competing map).

_doc: Sanskrit-logographic competing phoneme map for the Indus script. Same 12 anchored signs as parpola_phonemes.json, but with Sanskrit values instead of Dravidian. This is a SYNTHETIC reconstruction giving the Sanskrit hypothesis maximum benefit of the doubt: each sign is mapped to its most plausible Sanskrit lexical equivalent given the iconographic context. Used in Phase-30b T3 head-to-head falsification.
_methodology_note: Yajnadevam's actual claim is ALPHABETIC (76 allographs encoding Sanskrit phonemes a, i, u, ka, ra, etc. without iconic motivation). Under that strict alphabetic claim, iconographic anchors would score 0 because no sign would have a 'fish' or 'pot' meaning — they would just be sounds. To give Sanskrit the strongest possible test, this map adopts a LOGOGRAPHIC Sanskrit interpretation (similar to S.R. Rao 1982): each iconographically-anchored sign maps to its most plausible Sanskrit lexical equivalent. If even THIS lenient version fails to match Parpola's anchor score, the Sanskrit hypothesis is falsified.

47.source: Sanskrit cognate of Indo-Aryan fish; iconographic context
47.homonymy_note: Sanskrit 'matsya' has NO star-related homonymy; this is the key disadvantage vs Dravidian *miin* (fish/star). Astral compounds in Sanskrit use 'naksatra' or 'tara', neither of which has 'matsya' as a homophone.

311.source: Sanskrit 'nyagrodha' = banyan tree; cf. Atharva Veda. Note: Sanskrit also uses 'vaṭa' for banyan, but this is a Dravidian loanword (DEDR 5217); using 'nyagrodha' as the more 'native' Sanskrit term.
311.homonymy_note: Sanskrit 'nyagrodha' has NO directional homonymy with 'north'. The Dravidian rebus *vaṭa* = 'banyan' / 'north' / 'rope' is impossible to replicate in Sanskrit, where 'uttara' (north) shares no etymology with any tree-name.

16.source: Yajnadevam-style alphabetic assignment; common Indo-Aryan syllable

1.source: Sanskrit cognate; iconographic 'man with raised arm'
1.iconic: stick figure with one or both arms raised
1.homonymy_note: Sanskrit 'puruṣa' has no Dravidian-style aaL/vir homonymy with 'slave' or 'hero', but the 'man' meaning is preserved.

99.homonymy_note: Sanskrit 'dhanus' = 'bow' lacks the Dravidian 'vil' / 'vil-vaa-LL' (bow / bowman) compositional flexibility but the basic meaning is preserved.

117.gloss: wheel/discus; Sanskrit 'cakra' = wheel, discus, also Visnu's weapon
117.homonymy_note: Sanskrit 'cakra' has rich semantic field (wheel, discus, divine weapon, energy center). Strongest Sanskrit anchor.

124.homonymy_note: Sanskrit 'kumbha' is well-attested. Equally good fit as Dravidian *kuTam* — both are cross-linguistic IE/Dravidian water-pot terms.

87.gloss: two vertical strokes; Sanskrit 'śveta' = white/bright; cognate with Venus 'śukra'
87.source: Sanskrit cognate for white/bright; cf. śukra (Venus)
87.homonymy_note: Sanskrit 'śveta' = white, but Sanskrit Venus name is 'śukra' (cognate of Avestan haoma, sweet/bright). Sanskrit 'śveta-X' compounds for 'white star' are not idiomatic in the way Old Tamil 'veN-miin' is.

91.gloss: six vertical strokes; Sanskrit 'kṛttikā' = the Pleiades (six visible stars; six lokas)
91.source: Vedic / Puranic Sanskrit; 'kṛttikā' = the six Pleiades and the six wives of the Seven Sages
91.homonymy_note: Sanskrit 'kṛttikā' is independently attested for Pleiades, but the Indus compound 'six + fish' would have to be read as 'kṛttikā-matsya' which makes no Sanskrit sense (no compound gloss).

92.gloss: seven vertical strokes; Sanskrit 'saptarṣi' = Seven Sages = Ursa Major
92.homonymy_note: Sanskrit 'saptarṣi' is well-attested for Ursa Major, but the Indus compound 'seven + fish' would have to read as 'saptarṣi-matsya' which is not idiomatic Sanskrit.

175.gloss: spindle (no firm Sanskrit equivalent for radiance homonymy)
175.source: Lacks Sanskrit equivalent of Tamil 'katir' (spindle / radiance) homonymy

261.gloss: two intersecting circles; Sanskrit 'skanda' = the war-god, identified with Tamil Murukan
261.source: Sanskrit cognate via Vedic / Puranic identification
261.homonymy_note: Sanskrit 'skanda' lacks the 'ear-ring/bangle' homonymy that Dravidian *muruku* carries. The 'skanda' name is also a relatively LATE Vedic/Puranic synthesis (Murukan-Skanda fusion); pre-Aryan Indus inscriptions wouldn't carry this name natively.

281.gloss: five-striped palm squirrel; Sanskrit 'putra' = son, child
281.source: Sanskrit cognate for 'child' but lacks squirrel-iconography
281.homonymy_note: Sanskrit 'putra' = son/child but has NO connection to squirrels. Dravidian *piLLai* (squirrel / child) is precisely homophonic; Sanskrit 'putra' is not.

_buffalo_v2.homonymy_note: Sanskrit 'mahiṣa' is a strong cognate. Vedic 'Mahiṣa-asura' is Yama's mount; comparable royal-symbolism to Dravidian *erumai*.

_three_strokes_worlds.source: Sanskrit cognate; precise match to Parpola's Dravidian gloss

weakest_links_for_sanskrit_hypothesis: 47 fish: Sanskrit matsya has no star-homonymy (vs Dravidian miin/star). The single biggest disadvantage.
311 fig: Sanskrit nyagrodha has no north/rope homonymy (vs Dravidian vata/north).
281 squirrel: Sanskrit putra has no squirrel meaning (vs Dravidian piLLai/squirrel/child).
261 muruku: Sanskrit skanda is a late Vedic/Puranic name; Murukan-Skanda fusion is post-Indus.
strongest_links_for_sanskrit_hypothesis: 117 wheel = cakra (rich semantic field; common in Indus iconography too)
124 pot = kumbha (cross-linguistically attested; matches Parpola's reading equally well)
92 seven strokes = saptarsi / Ursa Major (Vedic astronomy)
